# Questions in Livestream 32

Live Stream #32: Last Stream for 2021

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp. The links use HTML anchors so GitHub can render them with `target="_blank"`.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KCxkCQlDTrY?t=321" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">5:21</a> | What advice would he give a medical doctor who wants to enter archaeology? | He said archaeology is much more research-driven than medical practice and usually requires moving on to a terminal research degree such as a PhD. | He explained that archaeology is less like practicing medicine on patients and more like being a doctor doing research. It can interface naturally with medical interests, especially mummy studies, but dig qualification generally requires a terminal research degree, heavy book work, and a strong appetite for scholarship. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KCxkCQlDTrY?t=475" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">7:55</a> | Is this really the last livestream of 2021? | Yes. He said he would not stream the next day, though Mr. Empty Conjecture might appear again. | He confirmed that it was really the last 2021 livestream because he was not planning to stream the next day and wanted time to himself and with family. He added lightly that Mr. Empty Conjecture might still make future appearances. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KCxkCQlDTrY?t=547" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">9:07</a> | Is nationhood a modern construct? | He said no. Ancient peoples had real national identities, even if the modern nation-state is a later form of that idea. | He rejected the idea that nationhood itself is only modern, saying ancient national identity existed and was often tied to ethnicity, agriculture, resource management, kingship, and civilization. He distinguished that from the modern nation-state, which he agreed is a different form of political identity. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KCxkCQlDTrY?t=667" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">11:07</a> | How were Yahweh's "three omnis" communicated to an ancient audience? | He pointed to creation, fulfilled prophecy, and divine judgment as ways scripture portrays God's power, knowledge, and goodness. | He identified the three classical attributes as omnipotence, omniscience, and omnibenevolence, while noting omnipresence belongs to a different conceptual category. In scripture, he said Yahweh's power is shown in creation, knowledge in foretelling the future, and goodness in blessing righteousness and judging as a holy God. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KCxkCQlDTrY?t=915" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">15:15</a> | What are his favorite anime and manga? | He named Mobile Suit Gundam, especially Thunderbolt and War in the Pocket, and said Your Lie in April was one of the best manga he had read recently. | He singled out Mobile Suit Gundam, including War in the Pocket and especially Thunderbolt, praising its animation, soundtrack, emotional weight, and ethical questions about the cost placed on soldiers. For manga, he highlighted Your Lie in April as heartbreaking and one of the best he had read recently. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KCxkCQlDTrY?t=1155" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">19:15</a> | How was human sacrifice practiced in Canaan? | He said it was typically child sacrifice, usually chosen by parents either as an appeal for prosperity or as a desperate act during siege conditions. | He described Canaanite human sacrifice chiefly as child sacrifice, not something normally assigned by an army. He distinguished prosperity sacrifices, using Carthage as an example, from desperate siege sacrifices, citing an Egyptian relief where a ruler sacrifices a son while under attack; in both cases he framed the decision as parental and often performed by knife. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KCxkCQlDTrY?t=1427" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">23:47</a> | Did ancient Egyptians view Canaanites and similar peoples as deeply wicked? | He said Egypt's view was strongly xenophobic: neighboring peoples were often treated as chaotic, inferior, and culturally lesser, though not always described in exactly biblical moral terms. | He said Egyptians did see neighboring peoples as vile, chaotic, savage, and culturally inferior, though he would not equate that exactly with biblical categories of debauchery or wickedness. He noted that smaller Levantine rulers were often treated as chiefs rather than equal kings, while great empires such as Hatti or Babylon could be addressed as peer powers. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KCxkCQlDTrY?t=1662" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">27:42</a> | What advice would he give a new Christian beginning theology studies in college? | He urged studying theology as an act of worship so it does not become spiritually dry or merely mechanical. | He welcomed the student and advised approaching theology as worship, not only as academic work. He said that posture helped keep study from becoming rote, mechanical, or spiritually dry, and later made returning to scripture feel worshipful rather than merely technical. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KCxkCQlDTrY?t=1767" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">29:27</a> | What did he think of the Jonathan Sheffield and C. J. Cox versus Josh Bowen and Maggie Bryson debate? | He thought all four did their best, but said the debate was disorganized, lacked subject-matter expertise, and did not get to the key Exodus issues. | He said he knew all four participants and thought they were trying hard, but he found the debate structure poor and the topic too unfocused. In his view, the best Exodus case would require expertise in Egyptology and careful attention to the relevant evidence, which the debate format did not provide. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KCxkCQlDTrY?t=1992" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">33:12</a> | What minimum level of technology was needed to build a pyramid? | He said not much by modern standards: bronze tools, sledges, boats, and practical quarrying methods were enough, even without wheels or common iron use. | He said pyramid building required no extraordinary technology beyond practical Old Kingdom tools and organization: bronze tools, stone pounders, sledges, boats, ropes, ramps, and quarrying skill. He emphasized that wheels and iron were not necessary and that the main challenge was labor organization and moving stone efficiently. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KCxkCQlDTrY?t=2278" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">37:58</a> | Do we find Egyptian pottery when Israel took over in Canaan, and if not, why not? | He said not much outside garrison contexts. Egypt mainly used Canaan to extract goods, so local pottery traditions usually remained dominant. | He said Egyptian pottery appears in some Egyptian garrison and administrative contexts, but it is not generally widespread through Canaan. Because Egypt was mostly extracting tribute and resources rather than replacing local daily life, Canaanite pottery traditions continued to dominate the material record. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KCxkCQlDTrY?t=2434" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">40:34</a> | When do specifically Israelite artifacts begin appearing in Canaan? | He said the earliest clear material is mostly pottery near the end of the Late Bronze Age, with the transition from Canaanite forms appearing gradually rather than all at once. | He cautioned that the evidence is not a sudden material break, since early Israelite pottery develops out of Canaanite forms. He placed the clearer signals around the end of the Late Bronze Age and early Iron Age, with collared-rim storage jars and highland settlement patterns becoming important indicators. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KCxkCQlDTrY?t=2669" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">44:29</a> | Did he watch Kip on MythVision with Josh critiquing one of his Q&A videos, and what did he think? | He said they quote-mined and straw-manned an informal livestream answer, especially by expecting him to cover issues he was not addressing. | He said he watched enough to think the critique was built around an informal Q&A answer treated as if it were a formal presentation. His objection was that they lifted snippets, imposed expectations from a different argument, and criticized him for not addressing issues that were outside the question he had been answering. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KCxkCQlDTrY?t=2839" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">47:19</a> | What did he think of the recent CT scan of King Amenhotep I? | He thought it was excellent technology, comparing it to virtual unrolling of scrolls and saying it could preserve artifacts while revealing hidden text. | He praised the scan as an impressive non-destructive tool and compared it to technology that can virtually unroll fragile scrolls. He was especially interested in its ability to reveal hidden information while preserving the original object instead of physically opening or damaging it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KCxkCQlDTrY?t=2990" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">49:50</a> | What input or resources did he have for young-earth and old-earth questions in a church apologetics team? | He did not recommend specific resources, said he has concerns with every major origins position, and noted both scientific and exegetical reservations. | He did not offer a resource list because he said he has reservations about every major origins model. He framed the issue as involving both scientific and exegetical problems, said he is not strongly committed to one camp, and suggested the topic requires humility because each position has tradeoffs. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KCxkCQlDTrY?t=3182" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">53:02</a> | Why are pyramids more famous ancient structures than ziggurats? | He said pyramids are larger, more visually striking, have internal and surrounding structures, and capture the imagination more than mud-brick ziggurats. | He explained that Egyptian pyramids are visually dramatic stone monuments with internal passages, mortuary temples, boat pits, and associated complexes, while ziggurats were generally mud-brick platforms that survive less impressively. He also said pyramids have a distinctive form and cultural mystique that draws more public attention. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KCxkCQlDTrY?t=3655" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:00:55</a> | How did the Israelites understand prophets, given passages like 1 Kings 22 with a class of prophets not necessarily sent by God? | He distinguished professional prophetic schools from prophets uniquely called by God, while noting God could still use someone trained in a prophetic school. | He described a difference between professional prophetic schools or guilds and individuals specially called by God. In passages like 1 Kings 22, he said a person could belong to the prophetic profession without necessarily speaking God's true message, though God could still call or use someone from that setting. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KCxkCQlDTrY?t=3935" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:05:35</a> | Are there good Orthodox Jewish scholars on the Exodus and conquest of Canaan? | He recommended Joshua Berman as an excellent Orthodox Jewish scholar, including on the Exodus, conquest, and documentary hypothesis. | He recommended Joshua Berman as a strong Orthodox Jewish scholar who has written on the Exodus, conquest, and documentary hypothesis. He said Berman is worth reading because he engages the critical material seriously while writing from within an Orthodox Jewish framework. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KCxkCQlDTrY?t=4105" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:08:25</a> | What should a theology or history student read next after reading his book? | He mentioned the Five Views on the Exodus volume, Paul J. Lawrence's Egg Whites or Turnips, and Behind the Scenes of the Old Testament. | He recommended moving into works that expose readers to multiple positions and broader background, including the Five Views on the Exodus volume, Paul J. Lawrence's Egg Whites or Turnips, and Behind the Scenes of the Old Testament. His emphasis was on building context rather than relying only on one author's argument. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KCxkCQlDTrY?t=4649" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:17:29</a> | Were Greeks inhabiting or trading with Egyptians before the Hellenization of Egypt? | Yes. He cited Naukratis in the seventh century BC and broader earlier trade evidence, including scarabs and possible Ramesses III-era contacts. | He said Greeks were interacting with Egypt well before Hellenistic rule, especially through trade and settlement. He cited Naukratis as a seventh-century BC Greek settlement in Egypt and mentioned earlier trade indicators such as scarabs and possible Ramesses III-period contacts. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KCxkCQlDTrY?t=4928" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:22:08</a> | In Numbers 31:17, why were the males not spared along with the women who tempted Israel during the war with Midian? | He framed it in terms of ancient vendetta and blood revenge, saying the passage is about eliminating both the sin and future retaliation. | He interpreted the passage through ancient concepts of vendetta and blood revenge. In that world, he said surviving males could become future avengers, so the command is presented as eliminating both the source of the immediate sin and the possibility of later retaliatory violence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KCxkCQlDTrY?t=5285" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:28:05</a> | Who are his top five favorite Egyptologists, and what are favorite works by them? | He named figures including Kenneth Kitchen, Mark Lehner, Barry Kemp, David Wilkinson, and Manfred Bietak, highlighting Kitchen's Ramesside inscriptions and Bietak's work at Avaris. | He named several favorite Egyptologists and associated them with major contributions: Kenneth Kitchen for Ramesside inscriptions and historical work, Mark Lehner for pyramids, Barry Kemp for settlement and Amarna work, David Wilkinson for early Egypt, and Manfred Bietak for Avaris and the Hyksos. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KCxkCQlDTrY?t=5478" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:31:18</a> | Was he planning a November 2022 video for the hundredth anniversary of Tutankhamun's tomb discovery? | He had not been planning one, but thought it was an excellent idea because the discovery changed Egyptology, archaeology, and biblical archaeology. | He said he had not been planning such a video, but the suggestion struck him as a strong idea. He described the discovery of Tutankhamun's tomb as transformative for Egyptology, archaeology, and biblical archaeology because of its public impact and the scale of intact material it revealed. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KCxkCQlDTrY?t=5578" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:32:58</a> | Could the Torah have originally been written in Egyptian, possibly hieroglyphs, and later translated into Hebrew? | He thought not, because the Torah's syntax looks Semitic rather than Egyptian, though it contains many Egyptian words. | He argued against an Egyptian original because the Torah's syntax and grammar look Semitic, not like translated Egyptian. He allowed that the text contains many Egyptian loanwords and cultural terms, but said vocabulary influence is different from an Egyptian-language original. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KCxkCQlDTrY?t=5898" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:38:18</a> | How did teenagers act and behave in the ancient Near East? | He said teenagers were treated more like adults, with adult responsibilities, though Egyptian society still distinguished childhood from later stages of life. | He said ancient Near Eastern teenagers generally entered adult responsibilities much earlier than modern teenagers, including work, marriage expectations, and household duties. At the same time, he noted Egyptian evidence still recognizes childhood and stages of life, so they were not simply invisible as a category. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KCxkCQlDTrY?t=6190" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:43:10</a> | Is there anything in Egyptology that sheds light on the lion-men of Moab in 2 Samuel 23:20? | He saw a possible royal-inscription parallel in lion imagery for kings and warriors, but treated it cautiously rather than as an exact Egyptian match. | He treated the connection cautiously, saying Egyptian material can show lions as royal or warrior imagery but does not provide a direct explanation of the Moabite phrase. He suggested the biblical wording may reflect heroic combat language or a title-like image rather than a specific Egyptian institution. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KCxkCQlDTrY?t=6525" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:48:45</a> | Has he ever tried to write a fictional story or novel? | Yes. He said he has written several novels, enjoys fiction as a creative outlet, and hopes someday to write a good one. | He said he has written several novel-length works and sees fiction as one outlet among many creative interests, including poetry, songwriting, painting, ceramics, and other arts. He keeps fiction separate from his nonfiction projects and hopes eventually to finish something he considers good. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KCxkCQlDTrY?t=6689" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:51:29</a> | Did the Egyptians have an eschatology? | He said they had an afterlife eschatology but not an end-of-days one; they expected the world to continue eternally. | He distinguished eschatology about life after death from final end-of-days eschatology. Egyptians, he said, had a strong afterlife vision involving trials in the Duat and transformation into a divine-like state, but no Revelation-like final end; they expected heaven and earth to continue forever. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KCxkCQlDTrY?t=6870" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:54:30</a> | In Genesis 46:34, was shepherding an occupation associated with Levantines, and was Egyptian dislike of shepherds an example of xenophobia? | He said yes: Egyptians associated sheep and goat herding with Levantine Semites, preferred cattle, and showed broader xenophobia toward Levantines. | He said Egyptians associated sheep and goat herding with Levantine Semites, while native Egyptians preferred cattle and viewed goat herding as polluting waterways. He connected this dislike to broader Egyptian xenophobia, including border controls and anti-Levantine hostility shown in some iconographic damage. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KCxkCQlDTrY?t=7030" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:57:10</a> | What are the earliest ancient Egyptian archaeological finds outside the ancient Near East? | He said the answer is difficult, but pointed to scattered scarabs, Hittite correspondence from Ramesses II in Turkey, and only limited material beyond the Near East. | He said the question is hard because the boundaries of the ancient Near East are themselves fuzzy. He mentioned Egyptian scarabs in Greece, Ramesses II-era correspondence recovered in Turkey, and a few possible finds around places such as Carthage or Spain, but stressed that beyond the Near East the material is sparse and mostly small objects. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KCxkCQlDTrY?t=7326" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:02:06</a> | How does knowing that a story follows an ancient pattern, such as patriarch stories following ancestor-epic patterns, affect interpretation? | He said patterns can help readers understand texts on ancient terms, but they do not by themselves prove fiction, borrowing, or any single conclusion. | He said ancient patterns can help readers interpret a text according to its own ancient framework, but they should not be used simplistically to decide fiction versus nonfiction. Using Troy and Moses basket traditions as examples, he argued that patterns may signal reception history, foreshadowing, or shared cultural forms without proving plagiarism, invention, or one fixed conclusion. |
